result:
# The Curator of Lost Hours

The clock repair shop on Meridian Street doesn't open at any particular time. Mrs. Chen has been walking past it for seventeen years, and she's never seen a pattern. Sometimes the lights glow amber at dawn. Sometimes the door remains locked until twilight. Once, she's certain, it stayed shuttered for an entire week, though her husband insists it was only three days.

Today, it's open at 2:47 PM.

She knows this because she's been checking her watch obsessively since the diagnosis. Stage three. Six months, maybe eight. Time, suddenly, has become the only thing that matters, and paradoxically, the only thing she cannot control.

The bell above the door releases a sound that isn't quite a chime. More like a sigh. Inside, the shop breathes with the accumulated tick-tick-ticking of hundreds of clocks, none of them synchronized, creating a rainfall of seconds that fall at different speeds.

Behind the counter sits a young man who looks exactly like the old man who was there when she first passed this shop in 2007. Same wire-rimmed glasses. Same ink stains on his fingers.

"I need more time," she says, then laughs bitterly at herself. "Sorry. I don't know why I came in. I don't even have a broken watch."

The man smiles gently. "Most people who come here don't."

He gestures to the forest of timepieces surrounding them. Grandfather clocks with faces painted like moons. Pocket watches dangling from invisible threads. Wristwatches arranged in spiral patterns on velvet boards. Digital displays showing times from time zones that might not exist.

"I collect lost hours," he says. "The ones that slip between moments. The hours people waste, the minutes they wish they could take back, the seconds they didn't notice passing."

"That's not how time works."

"Isn't it?" He stands, moving to a shelf crowded with small glass vials. Each one seems to contain something between liquid and light. "This one is from a Tuesday in March, 2003. Someone spent it arguing about something they can't even remember now. This one—" he touches another "—is a Sunday morning someone meant to call their mother but didn't. She died that Thursday."

Mrs. Chen feels her throat tighten. "You can't sell lost time back to people."

"No," he agrees. "But I can show you where yours went."

He pulls out a leather-bound ledger, flips through pages dense with handwriting in various inks, and stops. His finger traces down a column.

"You've lost 43 hours to traffic in the last year. 127 hours to scrolling through your phone looking at nothing in particular. 89 hours to worrying about things that never happened."

"Stop."

"You've lost 312 hours to sleep you didn't need, used as escape. 67 hours to conversations with people you don't like about subjects you don't care about. 891 hours—"

"I said stop."

But her voice is weak, because she wants to know. Needs to know.

"891 hours to thinking about painting again, but never picking up a brush."

The shop seems to contract around her. All those clocks, all those stolen seconds, pressing in.

"I was going to," she whispers. "After I retired. After my husband's business stabilized. After the kitchen renovation. After—"

"After," the man says softly, "is where hours go to die."

He closes the ledger, and when he looks at her, his eyes are impossibly old. Ancient. Sad.

"I can't give you more time, Mrs. Chen. That's not what I do. But I can show you what you do with what you have left."

He picks up a clock from the counter—a simple thing, wooden, with a white face and black hands. He turns it around. The back is a mirror.

"Every moment you're given has two faces," he says. "The one that passes, and the one you choose to meet it with. You can spend your six months, your eight months, your whatever-you-have thinking about the time you wasted. Or—"

He leaves the thought unfinished.

Mrs. Chen looks at her reflection in the back of the clock. She looks tired. Gray. Faded, like a photograph left too long in sunlight.

Then she notices something. In the reflection, just over her shoulder, she can see one of the shop's windows. Through it, the street outside glows with late afternoon sun, golden and thick as honey. How had she not noticed that light when she walked in?

"The art supply store," she says suddenly. "On Fifth. Is it still there?"

The man nods.

"What time do they close?"

He glances at the chaos of clocks around them, all telling different truths. "For you? They're open as long as you need them to be."

Mrs. Chen realizes she's crying, but it feels different than it has for the past three weeks since the diagnosis. Lighter somehow. Like something releasing rather than breaking.

"How much do I owe you?"

"You already paid," he says. "Everyone does. The question is what you'll buy with what's left."

The bell-sigh sounds again as she opens the door. The golden light hits her face, and she remembers—really remembers—what it felt like to be young and standing before a blank canvas, terrified and exhilarated, with nothing but possibility ahead.

Behind her, in the shop, the young-old man makes a note in his ledger, then carefully places a new vial on the shelf. Inside it, something luminous swirls.

It's labeled: *One afternoon in autumn. Found, not lost. Price: Paid in full.*
